title: Management of acute decompensated cirrhosis
one_line_summary: This review outlines systematic, evidence-based management of acute decompensated cirrhosis (AD), focusing on early identification of precipitants, infection control, renal protection, hemodynamic resuscitation, and nutritional optimization.
key_pearls: Perform diagnostic ascitic tap immediately for any patient with ascites, without waiting for coagulation correction.
Administer parenteral thiamine (>= 72h if Wernicke's suspected; 24h if not) before glucose-containing fluids in alcohol-related liver disease.
For SBP, administer 20% Albumin at **1.5 g**/kg at diagnosis and **1 g**/kg on Day 3 for renal protection.
In AKI, stop all NSAIDs, diuretics, and beta-blockers; for HRS-AKI, consider Terlipressin (2-**8 mg**/day) plus 20% Albumin (**100 mL** BID).
For AVH, administer Terlipressin (**2 mg** IV bolus, then 1-**2 mg** QDS) and discontinue 24 hours post-endoscopy once hemostasis is achieved.
Maintain a restrictive transfusion strategy for AVH: transfuse PRBCs only if Hb < **70 g**/dL (target 70-**80 g**/dL); avoid FFP/platelets to correct labs.

sections.heading: Initial Assessment & The 6-Hour Care Bundle
sections.content: Patients with AD require admission to a specialist gastroenterology or hepatology ward and evaluation by a specialist within 24 hours. Initial diagnostic workup includes CBC, LFTs, coagulation, U&Es, venous lactate, and blood cultures; a full non-invasive liver screen is indicated if not previously performed. A diagnostic paracentesis must be performed in all patients with ascites within the first 6 hours to rule out SBP, regardless of symptoms or inflammatory markers. Coagulopathy and thrombocytopenia are not contraindications to an ascitic tap, and prophylactic correction is not required. Imaging includes a Doppler ultrasound of the abdomen for portal/hepatic vein thrombosis, HCC, and ascites, and a CT head for reduced consciousness or focal neurology.

sections.heading: Management of Alcohol-Related Complications
sections.content: Patients with alcohol-related liver disease (ARLD) and active consumption must be assessed for Wernicke's Encephalopathy (WE). Parenteral thiamine should be administered for at least 72 hours in those with WE features, or 24 hours without, before oral transition. Alcohol withdrawal should be monitored using validated tools (e.g., CIWA, GMAWS) and treated with symptom-triggered benzodiazepines. Due to accumulation in hepatic impairment, reduced doses of chlordiazepoxide or shorter-acting agents like lorazepam should be used.

sections.heading: Infection & Spontaneous Bacterial Peritonitis (SBP)
sections.content: Cirrhotic patients are highly susceptible to infections but may lack classic signs; empirical broad-spectrum antibiotics should be initiated only with high suspicion or clear sepsis signs, followed by rapid de-escalation. SBP is diagnosed with ascitic fluid PMN count > 250/mm3. Management includes prompt broad-spectrum antibiotics and renal protection with intravenous 20% albumin (**1.5 g**/kg at diagnosis, **1 g**/kg at 72 hours), as SBP increases HRS risk. Routine albumin is not for non-SBP infections. Secondary prophylaxis (e.g., co-trimoxazole **960 mg** daily) should start immediately after acute antibiotic course completion.

sections.heading: Acute Kidney Injury (AKI) & Hepatorenal Syndrome (HRS-AKI)
sections.content: AKI affects 20-30% of AD patients, with etiologies including pre-renal (47%), ATN (32%), and HRS (23%). Initial AKI management involves suspending diuretics, beta-blockers, NSAIDs, and other nephrotoxics, and correcting hypovolemia with crystalloid boluses (**250 mL**) targeting UO > **0.5 mL**/kg/hr. HDU escalation is needed for persistent hypotension, UO < **0.5 mL**/kg/hr, rising lactate, or respiratory failure after 6 hours. HRS-AKI is diagnosed in AD patients with ascites meeting AKI criteria, failing volume expansion (after stopping nephrotoxics and treating infections), and lacking structural renal disease. Pharmacotherapy includes Terlipressin (2-**8 mg** daily, titrated) combined with 20% albumin (**100 mL** BID). Terlipressin requires extreme caution in patients > 70 years, with peripheral vascular disease, ischemic heart disease, or severe renal/liver impairment due to ischemic, respiratory failure, and sepsis risks.

sections.heading: Acute Variceal Haemorrhage (AVH)
sections.content: All upper GI bleeds in cirrhotic patients should be treated as AVH until proven otherwise. Resuscitation targets include a restrictive transfusion strategy for stable patients (Hb < **70 g**/dL, target 70-**80 g**/dL) and activating major hemorrhage protocol for instability (MAP > **65 mmHg**). Routine FFP and platelet transfusions should be avoided outside major hemorrhage protocols as they increase portal pressure. Tranexamic acid and PPIs are ineffective in AVH. Pre-endoscopy pharmacotherapy includes vasoactive agents like Terlipressin (**2 mg** IV bolus, then 1-**2 mg** QDS) or Octreotide (**50 mcg** IV bolus, then **50 mcg**/hr infusion if terlipressin **contraindicated**); terlipressin should be stopped 24 hours post-endoscopy. Empirical IV antibiotics and IV erythromycin (**250 mg**) pre-endoscopy are also **recommended**. Endoscopy should be performed within 24 hours, with intubation considered for active hematemesis, severe encephalopathy, or hemodynamic instability.

sections.heading: Hepatic Encephalopathy (HE)
sections.content: HE is a clinical diagnosis; serum ammonia levels are not required, though a normal level can help rule it out. Management involves identifying and treating precipitants (dehydration, hypokalemia, infection, constipation, sedatives). First-line treatment is lactulose, titrated to 2-3 soft stools daily; phosphate enemas can be used for severe constipation. Nasogastric tube placement for lactulose may be needed for patients unable to swallow, and Grade 3 or 4 HE requires **critical** care for airway protection. Rifaximin is reserved for secondary prevention of recurrent/chronic HE and does not acutely improve active episodes.

sections.heading: Ascites, Nutrition, and Thromboprophylaxis
sections.content: Ascites management includes moderate dietary salt restriction and therapeutic paracentesis for tense ascites. For non-tense ascites or peripheral edema, diuretics (Spironolactone **100 mg** daily + Furosemide **40 mg** daily, titrated every 72 hours) are used to target 0.5-**1.0 kg** daily weight loss, adjusted for renal function and potassium. Malnutrition is **critical**; provide a high-protein diet (**1.5 g**/kg/day) with small, frequent meals and a bedtime snack, aggressively replacing potassium, magnesium, and phosphate. VTE prophylaxis with tinzaparin is required for all patients with a platelet count > 50 x 10^9/L in the absence of active bleeding, as standard coagulation parameters are unreliable.

sections.heading: Severe Alcohol-Associated Hepatitis (AH)
sections.content: Corticosteroid therapy (Prednisolone **40 mg** daily) is indicated for severe alcohol-associated hepatitis (AH), determined by prognostic scores like Maddrey's Discriminant Function or MELD, after excluding active infection and GI bleeding. Response is assessed using the Lille Score at Day 4-7; if a non-responder (Lille score > 0.45), corticosteroids should be stopped immediately. Responders complete a 28-day course.

sections.heading: Deterioration & Discharge Planning
sections.content: Patients with ACLF and multi-organ failure require **critical** care referral. Urgent liver transplant assessment may be considered for patients < 60 years old with 3-organ failure, no transplant contraindications, and (for ARLD) at least 3 months of documented abstinence. To reduce readmissions, a structured discharge bundle should ensure enrollment in an early post-discharge clinic, clear self-management plans, direct liver team contact, alcohol abstinence support, and advanced care planning.

strengths_limitations: Strengths include a highly structured, rapid-response 6-hour care bundle with clear objective thresholds for interventions (e.g., PMN > 250/mm3 for SBP, Hb < **70 g**/dL for transfusion, Platelets > 50 x 10^9/L for VTE prophylaxis). Limitations involve low real-world implementation (only 11.4% in UK audits) and **significant** ischemic/respiratory risks with vasoactive therapies like terlipressin, requiring intensive monitoring and limiting use in older or cardiovascularly compromised patients.
